data.title: Chart Of The Day: Is This A 'Sleeper Sector' For 2026?
data.description:
By Mike Larson

I’m always on the lookout for “Sleeper Sectors” - groups of stocks that may not be getting talked about much, but that might have a surprise catalyst brewing. Today, let’s look at homebuilders!

This is my first MoneyShow Chart of the Day, which shows the average rate on a 30-year fixed mortgage going back three years. As you can see, we’re getting close to breaking the 6% threshold to the downside. We’re also right around a three-year low.

Chart #1: 30-Year Mortgage Rates

(Source: Freddie Mac)

Now here’s my second MoneyShow Chart of the Day, which shows a much longer-term chart of existing home sales. You can see the huge dip during the pandemic, the big rebound afterward, the sickening slump that followed... and the long period of sideways chop since 2023.

Chart #2: Existing Home Sales

(Source: Trading Economics)

If you look closely at the last few months, though, you can also see that we’ve seen a persistent pickup in sales. Falling financing costs, solid-if-not-spectacular wage growth, and price stagnation in many parts of the country are helping restore a semblance of affordability to homes again. And buyers are starting to notice.

As for homebuilding stocks? Well, the State Street SPDR S&P Homebuilders ETF (XHB) is up in the past year... but only 7.3%. The iShares US Home Construction ETF (ITB) is up just 4.2%. Both are badly lagging the 19.9% rise in the SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY) during the same 12-month period.

XHB also owns building supply companies, home furnishing retailers, home improvement stores, and other derivative stocks. So, it’s not technically a “pure-play” home builder ETF. But ITB is much more heavily weighted toward builders like D.R. Horton Inc. (DHI) and Lennar Corp. (LEN)... and even it hasn’t moved much.

So, I think the Sleeper Sector name applies here. And I think you may want to investigate these stocks and funds further for 2026.

data.title: Goldman Sachs Stock Is Running A Bit Hot
data.description:
It seems that I may have turned neutral on The Goldman Sachs Group, Inc. (GS) too early. Since I downgraded the stock in mid-November, it has advanced by another ~22%. In that analysis, I viewed the fundamentals as stellar, but due to valuation concerns, I decided that some caution was warranted. Goldman Sachs reported their latest earnings just today (Jan 15th), and so we'll analyze the results and valuation to see whether changes to my stance are necessary now.

Below, it is shown that Goldman Sachs is getting back to its roots, and that is helping with their performance. Their flagship Global Banking & Markets segment is leading the way, and strong inflows show that their push into Asset & Wealth Management is strong. While prospects for the business are high right now, I would say that the valuation may be running a bit too hot. The P/B ratio is miles above its historic median and trades at a considerable premium to the financial sector. Therefore, while I'm highly bullish on Goldman Sachs the company, I have decided to stick with my hold rating for the stock.

Their Strategy at a Glance

Before getting to the financials, I believe it is worth mentioning that the firm's shift back to its roots has been a great strategic decision. Goldman Sachs has been a premier investment bank for decades, and so in hindsight, the push into consumer areas with their Marcus platform and the Apple Card (AAPL) were missteps. As you can see above, they have narrowed their strategic focus and are back to doing what they do best. This relatively quick pivot in strategy seems to be paying off.

In addition, the company is very focused on growing their Asset & Wealth Management business, seemingly following in the footsteps of their arch-rival Morgan Stanley (MS). As stated above, this can lead to more durable revenues over time, and that could help to remove some of the cyclicality in their business, which in turn could boost their valuation. Overall, this brief discussion is meant to show that Goldman Sachs has been wise to get back to their roots.

Q4 Results Overview

Let's take a broad view of their earnings before looking closer at their segment results a little later. For 2025 Q4, Goldman Sachs generated total revenues of $13.454 billion. That represents a 3% decline YoY, and they missed consensus expectations of $13.85 billion. However, investors need not be concerned, as they stated this was a result of a $2.26 billion Apple Card markdown in their Platform Solutions segment. Therefore, moving forward, no such drag on performance should be present. As shown on the right, Global Banking & Markets saw strong growth, but their Asset & Wealth Management results may have been a bit weaker than desired as there was a YoY drop in revenues.

In terms of business efficiency, things perhaps could have been a little better. Operating expenses surged by 18% YoY as they reported higher compensation and benefits as well as higher transaction expenses. In terms of profitability, you can see that EPS growth was strong at 17% and that ROE and ROTE expanded both YoY and QoQ. However, note that while the Apple Card transition negatively affected revenues, the net benefit seen in provision for credit losses was a result of a $2.48 billion reserve reduction related to Apple Card. This benefit more than offsets the markdown effects on revenue, and so bottom line figures and profitability ratios are a good reflection of where the firm is really at right now. While Goldman Sachs' top line missed, they beat EPS expectations by $2.25, and so Q4 was overall a solid quarter in my view.

Global Banking & Markets

Goldman Sachs' flagship segment did the heavy lifting in Q4 with net revenues growing by 22%. Investment banking fees were very robust, and that is a pleasant surprise given that my recent analysis on JPMorgan (JPM) showed that activity was a bit sluggish. That said, you can see that equity underwriting saw just 4% growth, and that may be a reflection of how the U.S. Government shutdown affected IPOs. In terms of sales and trading, the firm beat expectations for both FICC and equities. I believe the strength in equities was a key highlight of their Q4 and is likely a reflection of an increase in volatility, particularly in tech stocks, back in November.

It is worth mentioning that M&A advisory will be a core growth driver moving forward. As you can see above, Goldman Sachs has been on top in this space, and recently it was reported that in 2025, the firm advised on 38 of 68 $10 billion+ deals. This shows that they are a clear leader. Due to the forward catalysts listed above, their backlog is now at its highest level in four years, and that suggests to me that M&A will be a key area of focus moving forward.

Asset & Wealth Management

While the headline revenue drop of 1% YoY may be disappointing, a more in-depth look at their AUS shows that this business continues to strengthen. As shown on the right, total net inflows were considerably more than in the previous quarter and the prior year period, as there were strong long-term AUS inflows. In addition, they also saw a decent amount of net market appreciation, which is an indication that their investment performance is solid.

One of the greatest areas of potential within this segment would be alternatives. At the end of their Q4, alternative investments had $420 billion in AUS, and it is shown in the above infographic that they are targeting $750 billion in AUS by 2030. This shows that they are significantly upping their game in this space, and that should be a driver of fee growth moving forward. Alternatives have been, overall, a lucrative area of investments in the financial sector in general, and so Goldman Sachs' push into this area is a promising move in my view.

Valuation Running Hot

Goldman Sachs' fundamentals are currently solid, and their strategy is back to where it should be. The company has some good growth drivers in each of their key segments, and so the outlook does seem to be strong for the firm. That said, the valuation may be running a bit too hot. As you can see in the chart above, their P/B ratio is currently at 2.663, and that is miles above the 5-year median of just 1.305. Furthermore, Seeking Alpha shows that their P/B is at a 106% premium to the Financials sector and overall their valuation is rated as a D-. Therefore, as of this writing, the stock isn't cheap. Goldman Sachs is back to what they do best, and they are seemingly focused on expanding their Asset & Wealth Management business, but in my view, the valuation indicates that there is a bit too much optimism and that investors may be overpaying for strong fundamentals.

Conclusion

I'm very bullish on Goldman Sachs, the company. With Apple Card now behind them, the company is clearly focused on the right areas. The flagship segment is doing well, with M&A being a major opportunity, while inflows and alternative AUS targets show that they are aggressively expanding in the wealth management area as well. Fee growth could help to remove some of the cyclicality in their business. While this does justify a premium over the sector and the historic median, I believe the P/B ratio's now highly elevated level shows that opportunity in the stock is quite limited. Therefore, while the prospects are bright for Goldman Sachs, I have decided to reiterate my hold rating.

data.title: Tracking The Rise Of Islamic Finance
data.description:
In January 2026, the FTSE Yasaar Global Equity Shariah Index Series celebrates its 20th anniversary.

Over the two decades of its existence, this equity benchmark family—established in 2006 via a partnership between FTSE Russell and specialist Shariah consultants Yasaar Limited—has established itself as a key part of the fast-growing Islamic Finance industry. Today, the number of indices within the FTSE Yasaar Shariah Index family exceeds 150 and continues to grow.

Growth of Islamic Finance

In a recent report, the London Stock Exchange Group (LSEG) forecasts that Islamic finance assets worldwide will approach the $10trn mark by the end of this decade, a rapid rate of growth for a relatively new industry: Islamic finance assets only surpassed $1trn in 2010 and reached their second trillion in 2014.

The Islamic finance industry has now expanded its footprint to 140 countries, says LSEG, and its asset growth is not just a reflection of the growing savings of the world’s Muslim population.

While Muslim-majority countries in the Middle East and Southeast Asia continue to dominate the industry, developments and growth in other markets continue. This is in large part because of the intrinsically ethical nature of Shariah finance (Islamic equity indices screen out companies involved in conventional finance, alcohol and intoxicants, gambling, weapons, arms and defence manufacturing).

In particular, the United Kingdom, LSEG’s and FTSE Russell’s home market, is now a key hub for listings of green and sustainable sukuk (Islamic debt instruments).

Figure 1: Islamic finance assets growth

The FTSE Russell/Yasaar partnership

The FTSE Yasaar Global Equity Shariah Index Series is based on FTSE Russell’s flagship global equity benchmark, the FTSE Global Equity Index Series (FTSE GEIS). The starting set of companies included in the FTSE GEIS is then screened for Shariah compliance (compliant with the precepts of Islamic finance) by Yasaar.

Majid Dawood, Yasaar’s founder and chief executive, explains the distinctive features of an Islamic approach to equity investment.

“Islamic finance is about financing real growth with real money,” says Mr. Dawood.

“Money shouldn’t make money: building things should make money and selling things should make money.”

“One of the important ideas in Islam is that you cannot sell fruit from a tree before it has appeared. That’s because you don’t know what the result will be and whether the tree will produce fruit at all.”

“So Islamic finance is about selling what you have, not what you might have in future.”

These principles are embedded in the FTSE Yasaar Global Equity Shariah Index Series rules in two ways: business activity screening removes companies involved in undesired activities, and financial screening removes companies with excessive reliance on debt, interest or leverage.

Importantly, Yasaar has always had a conservative approach to screening: for example, it ensures Shariah compliance by rating debt ratio limits as a percentage of total assets (rather than as a percentage of trailing market capitalisation, which is a more volatile measure).

Yasaar’s screening process is also overseen and monitored by the company’s Shariah board, which reviews and audits the screening process at regular intervals. Shariah-compliant constituents are updated daily and audit trails or any changes to a company’s Shariah status are available to the Board for review.

Performance in line with the global equity market

The primary use cases of the FTSE Global Equity Shariah Index Series are as a reference benchmark for the performance of Shariah-compliant equity portfolios and as an underlying performance target for investment products that meet the requirements of Islamic investors.

If the investment performance of the indices within the series is secondary to these requirements, in practice the net financial impact on a global equity investor of preferring a Shariah approach has been marginal.

The total return of the FTSE Shariah All-World index over the two decades since its launch has been very much in line with that of the FTSE All-World index (the most widely used investable benchmark from the FTSE Global Equity Index Series).

Figure 2: Two decades of the FTSE Shariah All-World Index

Poised for further growth

As the FTSE Yasaar Global Equity Shariah Index Series embarks on its third decade, what lies ahead?

The prospects for the Islamic finance sector seem bright: Islamic finance services are now available in a range of markets around the world, the sector can now compete directly with its conventional counterparts, and there is healthy product innovation in the form of digital sukuk, Islamic fintech and further integration with other sustainable finance approaches.

The demand for indices and benchmarks that embed Islamic investment principles could therefore grow substantially further in the coming years.

data.title: Turn Oklo's Hype Into Income
data.description:
Over the last year or so, I’ve covered nuclear startup Oklo (OKLO) four times, at different points highlighting the company’s various strengths and weaknesses.

In December of 2024, I called shares a Strong Buy on the back of the company’s significant nuclear ambitions, attractive entry price point, and long-term potential.

With no serious technical hurdles to overcome, I viewed the company’s innovative engagement with the NRC’s regulatory process as bringing dynamism to a once-stagnant nuclear market. With an attractive business model to boot, I thought shares appeared underpriced.

Throughout 2025, the market validated my thesis, as shares eventually skyrocketed roughly 800% from my initial bullish call:

More recently, in late summer of 2025, I downgraded shares due to significant valuation concerns.

Sure, nuclear power might be the future, and Oklo might be the fastest, most well-capitalized mover in the market. But with significant execution hurdles, a long timeline for buildout, and economics that have yet to be proven, I viewed shares as too risky.

Fast forward to the present, and the company’s stock has remained volatile, more than doubling (and then halving) throughout the fall and winter season.

While management has done a good job executing, continuing to build momentum with new clients and plans for new facilities, shares are still priced at an exorbitant premium.

Thus, at the present moment, I view selling put options on the stock as the most attractive way to generate returns here.

Sure, you could buy and hold shares over the long term - and I expect that eventually you’d be rewarded - but for the time being, selling put options, generating a substantial cash-on-cash return, and retaining the chance to buy shares in the event of a significant dip looks like the best way to play it.

Today, I’ll lay out recent updates with OKLO, highlight the valuation, and explain why I believe selling out-of-the-money put options is an unbelievable risk-return trade right now.

Sound good? Let’s dive in.

Financials & Price

When it comes to Oklo, the company has no real financials to speak of, as the business does not yet produce any real revenues. As a design / development stage company, the firm has identified an attractive market segment and is building toward future commercial operations, but the business doesn’t yet make any money.

As a result, valuing the stock and analyzing the financials by any traditional means is relatively difficult.

This, I suspect, is responsible for a great deal of the volatility around the stock. As a business's prospects become less clear, the wider the potential range of outcomes, which has driven the wild moves.

Thus, with no real financials or traditional value metrics to go off of, three key questions linger over the business for investors:

  • First, is there significant demand for nuclear power?
  • Secondly, does the company have enough capital to 'get there'?
  • And finally, is the stock priced to allow for additional gains?

Let’s go through each of these a little bit more closely.

If you’ve read my previous articles on Oklo, then you know that I’m particularly bullish on nuclear energy. The U.S. faces a significant power supply gap between now and 2030, as increasing demands from AI, electric vehicles, and population growth overburden the current electrical grid.

At the same time, the world is attempting to go through a clean energy transition away from dirty fossil fuels. When it comes to power load stability, lack of emissions, and efficiency, nuclear power is the clear choice for society.

As old natural gas plants go offline in the next five to ten years - and our energy demands grow - no power source except for nuclear is poised to truly tackle the problem.

Additionally, increasing cyber threats from foreign countries make a large-scale national grid appear more vulnerable than ever. All of these ingredients add up to small nuclear reactors being the way forward. Providing localized, efficient, clean power to data centers, factories, hospitals, and regional interconnections makes small nuclear reactors appear like the most attractive energy generation option going forward.

The company has proven this out with its business model and recent contract wins. Oklo’s proposed business model vertically integrates the nuclear industry, putting construction, generation, and commercialization efforts all under the same roof:

First, our build-own-operate model allows us to sell power directly to customers under long-term contracts. That creates recurring revenue and streamlines the regulatory process by keeping ownership and operational control within Oklo.

Similarly, if the company manages to get its first fuel enrichment plant in Tennessee online, part of the nuclear supply chain will also be owned by Oklo.

At the same time, Meta’s recent power purchase agreement with Oklo indicates that there is significant demand from major energy users if and when the company can scale up its unique 'powerhouse' concept:

In short, at a base level, I believe that there is significant demand for nuclear power, and thus, there will be significant revenue potential for Oklo once it moves past the development stage and into generation.

Secondly, will the company have enough capital to get there?

Building nuclear reactors, even small ones, is a capital-intensive process, and getting past regulatory hurdles takes persistence, grit, and significant admin expense. The good news is that the company’s recent stock price rally has significantly eased the company’s financial burdens

As shares have skyrocketed throughout 2025, the company has announced two significant share sale programs for $1.5 billion and $3.5 billion, respectively. This allows the company to tap capital markets when it needs liquidity.

At the same time, the company’s balance sheet lists more than $1.2 billion in total assets, most of which remain liquid, with no real debt to speak of:

In the company’s early investor materials, management expected 50 MW powerhouses to cost roughly $142 million initially, dropping to $116 million per unit after efficiencies of scale kick in:

Even if costs turn out to be triple management's early expectations, there is still significant liquidity to build a number of functional powerhouses and make significant progress on the fuel enrichment plant planned in Tennessee.

Additionally, there’s no explicit timeline pressure provided by interest payments or debt coming due. As a result, I view significant development delays for Oklo to be less of a material negative than they would be for other development-stage companies. There’s a lot of flexibility here.

In sum, I believe that Oklo’s ongoing execution - including breaking ground on its first powerhouse in Idaho - and significant capital reserves make the company’s nuclear vision possible.

Finally, the third question is whether the stock is priced for additional gains in the event of a successful commercial launch.

This is where my opinion diverges from the market.

If you look at analyst estimates, they’re currently expecting the company to produce negative, but steadily improving, losses through 2029, until the company breaks even in 2030:

This is because Oklo does not plan to start bringing in revenues until 2027 or 2028, as the first powerhouse plant is proven out, regulatory decisions come through, and a more meaningful buildout and fulfillment of its PPA contracts can commence in earnest.

For my part, I mostly agree with this timeline. I don’t expect that the company will produce positive earnings until 2030. However, if you look at the unit economics per power plant, the stock appears unbelievably expensive at present.

If you assume a per-powerhouse annual revenue of roughly $36 million, ongoing expenses of $10 million per year, and the 4-5 year payback period, the return on capital is obviously attractive:

However, depending on how many powerhouses the company can produce by 2030, there’s significant variance in my gross profit expectations. If the company can achieve ten powerhouses by 2030, then you’re looking at roughly $350 million in annual revenue, which could produce an annual gross profit of $280 million.

This implies a 2030 gross profit multiple of roughly 54x. In other words, if the company only achieves ten powerhouses by 2030, then the stock appears incredibly, incredibly expensive. That's more expensive than Palantir (PLTR), for reference.

If the company can produce twenty powerhouses, then these numbers double, and the valuation halves to roughly 27 times gross profit. For a result that could be five-plus years away, this is still incredibly expensive.

As a result, even if the company can reach a successful commercial launch and produce dozens of powerhouses over the next five years (which I believe is ambitious), the stock already appears somewhat priced for that. Remember, at the end of the day, this is a utility business, and one that will eventually have significant regulation on, or volatility in, its rates.

As a result, I think shares look like a hold for now, even with the potential exponentiality of far, far off EPS estimates.

The Trade

So - owning Oklo could be a great long-term bet, but shares are too expensively priced to realize much upside in the short and medium term. With a setup like this, I view selling puts as the best risk-reward option for your money.

When you sell a put option, you’re telling the market that you’re willing to buy shares of a company at a certain strike price between now and the expiration of the option.

In return for committing to this, the buyer pays you a cash premium.

Essentially, you’re selling insurance that if the stock goes down, you’ll buy it off of someone at a guaranteed price.

Given that I’d love to own Oklo, but the high valuation presents an issue, selling puts is a beautiful way to capitalize on that view.

For Oklo, selling the May 15 $50 strike put options looks like the best available choice:

With 120 days between now and expiration, this trade keeps you involved with Oklo for roughly four months, and in my experience, I've found that 70 - 140 days is the 'sweet spot' for selling OTM options. This is because you get some flexibility (you're not locked in for years), and you can sell far out of the money while still generating a solid return. For shorter DTE contracts, the nominal yields are quite low.

With this trade, if the stock dips beneath $50, - which is roughly half the current price - then you’d be assigned shares of the company. As I described earlier, I believe that a much cheaper price offers a much better entry point, so this isn't necessarily a negative.

On the reward side, these options currently pay a credit of $245 per contract. Given that these options only require $4,755 in margin to hold in your brokerage account, selling these puts produces a cash-on-cash return of 5.15%, which annualizes to nearly ~16%.

This is a much better yield than you’ll get from bonds, high-dividend stocks, business development companies, MLPs, or nearly any other type of income-producing asset.

At the same time, you preserve your chance to buy Oklo at a significant discount.

If Oklo trades up, sideways, or down (but still above $50) over the next four months, then you get to keep the cash premium free and clear.

There are some risks to be aware of. On one hand, if OKLO's stock performs well, then selling puts doesn't capture as much of that upside, so there's potential opportunity cost here. Additionally, if OKLO's stock price craters over the next 4 months, then you could be stuck with OKLO over the long term, with significant capital impairment. This isn't a different risk profile than simply holding the stock, but it's worth being aware of.

Summary

Overall, though, I believe that selling put options in Oklo is the best risk-reward move investors can make with the stock right now.

The company’s prospects appear bright, but the stock price reflects that.

As a result, I don’t view shares as a compelling buy.

That said, selling put options with a 50% break-even and a 16% annualized yield over the next 120 days looks like a great win-win trade. Either you get your cash back free and clear, or you are assigned a great company at a better price than it’s currently trading.

Cheers~

data.title: PCQ: Even At A Pop Higher, Value In This Muni Fund Remains
data.description:
Main Thesis & Background

The purpose of this article is to evaluate the PIMCO California Municipal Income Fund (PCQ) as an investment option at the current market price. The fund invests primarily in California municipal bonds and therefore seeks to provide current income that is exempt from federal and California income tax. It seeks to be “AMT-free” by investing only up to 20% in bonds generating interest that may subject individuals to the alternative minimum tax.

About four months ago, I took another look at PCQ after being bullish on the fund for most of 2025. I noted how I was premature on my "buy" call for this fund, but reiterated that I saw a brighter path forward. Looking back, PCQ has indeed performed well. It has pumped out a gain of over 7%, and that has actually beaten the equity market since early September:

Clearly, this is a strong move, and it got me pondering if now is a good time to get a bit more cautious on PCQ. This is a fund with a history of volatility. After this type of gain, logic could dictate a turnaround is in the cards.

Fortunately, after review, I still have a positive take on this CEF. The fund's discount to NAV remains attractive, the backdrop for munis is solid, and equities are looking pricey—which could entice investors to look to munis as a hedge. For these reasons, I am keeping the "buy" rating in place and will explain why in detail below.

Despite Gain, Discount To NAV Still Wide

One of the primary reasons I don't see a need to downgrade PCQ right now is the fund's valuation. This is due to the fact that on the surface, the discount to NAV looks quite appealing. At exactly 11%, investors are getting a nice chance to buy this fund "on sale":

But it is more than just the surface view that entices me. Specifically, this number has actually widened (albeit slightly) since my September review. Now the differential may seem negligible since it was around 10% at that time. But it isn't the shift of 1% in isolation that is supportive of a bullish view on its own. It is the fact that this occurred while PCQ offered investors a 7% total return. Simply, this fund has gotten cheaper while delivering gains. That is a fairly rare thing in the world of CEFs.

The bottom line is that PCQ has been seeing its underlying securities appreciate quite handsomely in value. This means the fund still offers investors the same discount opportunity it did four months ago, even after a sharp short-term gain. This gives me confidence that the fund is invested in the right type of assets and is central to why I am keeping a bull view in place.

Income Story Remains Positive

A second positive attribute for PCQ is the distribution. This is naturally why most people will look to Munis overall—and PCQ by extension. As a California-focused muni CEF, offering a high rate of tax-exempt income will be critical to draw in investment dollars. Fortunately, that is precisely what this fund does.

At present, PCQ is offering a yield near 5%. When we account for state and federal taxes for high-income earners residing in the Golden State, that number climbs at least a few percentage points (this is dependent on your own personal tax situation):

Again, this isn't necessarily the right distribution stream for everyone. But for those who live in California and face above-average tax rates, this seems a bit of a no-brainer to me.

Of course, PCQ has a history of distribution cuts. So assessing the health of this income is also important. But the story is positive again here, too. The coverage metrics for the fund are in the 90% range, and this has been consistent since my article back in Q3 last year:

This all adds up to a positive story. PCQ has a high income stream, which is amplified by its tax benefits. I see this continuing to interest income-oriented investors in the year ahead, and readers would be wise to consider it going forward if the tax considerations make sense for them personally.

Maturities Benefit From Steep Yield Curve

A third reason to like PCQ in particular has to do with the maturity make-up of its holdings. What I mean here is that the fund is primarily invested in long-dated munis. While it does have about 18% of its assets tied to maturity dates of 5 years or less, over half of the fund is exposed to maturity dates in the 10-20+ year range, as illustrated in the graphic below:

So—why is this "good"? That answer, of course, depends on one's perspective. But for me, I view it positively primarily because that is where the yield advantage (over treasuries) really exists right now. To show why we need to look at the competing yield curves. We see that munis have a positive spread over treasuries across all maturity dates. But that differential really expands the further out on the curve we go:

What this shows is that the yield curve for munis is relatively very steep compared to treasuries. This signals attractiveness for muni yields in particular at the long end of the curve, and, as noted, this is where PCQ has the majority of its exposure.

The conclusion I draw from this is that those considering the muni market should be considering longer-dated securities for the maximum income spread benefit. One way to do that is to own PCQ. This reality helps me confidently keep a bullish rating in place going forward.

Munis A Good Option For An Expensive Market

My final point rests on why my followers should be considering munis at all. What I mean is - why would one want to be a buyer of munis at this time more broadly—whether through PCQ or any other vehicle?

The answer lies in my personal view of being less risk-on as 2026 gets underway. We had a record year in 2025, and plenty of risk-on assets soared. This included the S&P 500, but also non-US stocks and other commodities like copper and silver. Even some traditional "safe haven" assets like gold rallied, while muni bonds struggled by comparison.

What this tells me is that a turnaround could be in the cards for munis. In fact, it has really already begun, as we can see through PCQ's 7% pop since September. This is a sector that is as traditional as a defensive hedge as one can find, and I think the backdrop is especially favorable for that type of play.

Part of the rationale for this view has to do with valuations within equities. It should not surprise anyone to find the S&P 500 is quite pricey at the moment. Yes, P/E ratios can be elevated for a long time. And, yes, P/E ratios have been higher in the past. But that shouldn't discount the fact that they are above long-term averages right now. That makes me generally cautious. Further, this story extends beyond just the S&P 500. While emerging market equities are markedly cheaper, they too are above their own longer-term average:

The takeaway for me is that I see limited value across the equity realm at present. Does that make me a complete bear? Of course not. But it does tell me that now is a good time to be putting new cash to work in areas that aren't so expensive—especially compared to their historical ranges. Munis fit this bill perfectly, and readers can find plenty of value across this sector. This includes PCQ, but also a plethora of other funds if this particular CEF doesn't suit them.

Bottom Line

PCQ has delivered a strong return, but I see the potential for more gains. This CEF still sports a healthy discount to NAV, its monthly distribution stream is supported by net investment income, and the fund's long-dated securities are offering wide spreads against comparable treasuries. All of this adds up to a backdrop where a "buy" rating for PCQ still makes sense. I would therefore suggest that my followers give the idea some consideration at this time.

data.title: Las Vegas Sands: Fairly Valued, Healthy Outlook Ahead
data.description:
Las Vegas Sands Corp. (LVS) has reported strong overall earnings growth. The company’s market share losses in Macau have been more than offset by strong sector growth, and LVS’s Marina Bay Sands property in Singapore has continued to shine. Marina Bay Sands investments and good 2026 Macau projections make LVS’s earnings growth outlook good.

I initiated the stock at a Hold rating in my initial September 2024 article on the stock, titled “Las Vegas Sands: Chinese Stimulus And Rising Tourism Improve Short-Term Outlook”. The stock has since returned 21%, on par with the S&P 500.

Macau’s Casinos Continue to Show Strong Growth, But LVS Is Losing Market Share

LVS, namely the company’s Chinese subsidiary Sands China (SCHYY), has gained significantly from a continued surge in Macau. Across Macau, industry-wide gross gaming revenue grew by 9.1% during 2025. The year was characterized by moderate growth during the first months, accelerating significantly in latter months into rapid 14.8% growth in December.

Even the rapid December figure did slightly miss analysts' very ambitious expectations for the month. The growth rate is very good regardless.

The market is still below pre-Covid levels due to regulatory changes in the junket system that have lowered VIP spending structurally. Regardless, growth is a clear positive for casino operators in the market. Against a muted industry performance in Las Vegas, operating casinos has been much more lucrative in Macau in 2025. JPMorgan expects Macau’s gaming industry to continue on a relatively strong trajectory in 2026; GGR growth is estimated at 5-6% for the year, driven by mass market gains but a slower VIP performance. Macau's casino outlook is healthy.

Latest Q3 results by LVS show the impact of the industry tailwind in Macau. The company showed 7.6% overall revenue growth to $1.91 billion in Macau, split between very strong growth in The Londoner after renovations but a more subdued performance in LVS’s other Macau properties. Adjusted property EBITDA ended up at $601 million, up by 2.7% year-on-year.

The results do underline clear competitive intensity in Macau—despite solid overall growth, LVS’s growth clearly trailed the even better 12.5% industry GGR growth rate. Macau’s competitive environment is very challenging, as, for example, Galaxy Entertainment Group (GXYEF) and Wynn Resorts (WYNN) have made notable investments in the market. LVS’s growth compares to Wynn Resorts’ 14.8% revenue growth, Galaxy Entertainment’s 20% revenue growth, and MGM Resorts’ (MGM) 17% revenue growth in the Macau market. All showed significantly better growth than LVS.

Ultimately, LVS’s Macau outlook is twofold. The overall Macau market is still expected to show very solid growth in at least 2026, bolstering LVS’s revenue outlook in the market. The 2026 market share outlook should also be aided by LVS’s renovations in The Londoner, finished in Q2’2025. Yet, the company has lost market share in the past few years as competitors seem to have invested more in the market—over the longer term, LVS’s market share in Macau isn’t cemented.

Marina Bay Sands’ Outlook Impresses

LVS’s flagship Singaporean property is performing incredibly well. Marina Bay Sands latest showed massive 56.3% revenue growth to $1436 million in Q3, leading the property’s adjusted EBITDA to surge by 83.0% to $743 million. Approximately $179 million of Marina Bay Sands’ $517 million in revenue growth was caused by an extraordinarily high fluctuation in the hold percentage, but underlying 33.0% hold-adjusted revenue growth was incredibly good as well.

Despite tourist arrivals in Singapore not growing very much, tourists continue to spend more in the country. The trend has boded well for Marina Bay Sands.

On top, LVS continues to invest heavily in the property. The company finished suite renovations in Marina Bay Sands in Q2 2025, but has started an even more ambitious $8 billion expansion project in mid-2025. The expansion project is estimated to open at the start of 2031, and includes an ultra luxury hotel with 570 suites, a 15,000-seat live entertainment arena, and other amenities.

Given Marina Bay Sands’ current success, such heavy expansion investments seem to be warranted. The property’s long-term outlook is incredibly good.

Implications for Upcoming Q4 Report

LVS is expected to report its Q4 results in late January. Wall Street expects revenues of $3.32 billion and an adjusted EPS of $0.76 in the upcoming report. The consensus estimate implies 14.7% year-on-year revenue growth for the quarter - LVS's good overall performance is expected to last. The adjusted EPS is estimated to show 40.0% growth, implicating noteworthy margin expansion.

I believe that such estimates are very fair. Macau's casino industry GGR even accelerated by 250 basis points from Q3 to Q4 to 15.0%, but Wall Street expects LVS's growth to slow down sequentially from 24.2% in Q3. A weaker hold percentage in Marina Bay Sands is likely to make overall growth slower in Q4 than in Q3. Leverage in operating expenses makes margin expansion likely, especially on the net income level.

Earnings are expected to stay similar on a sequential basis.

The main focus point is LVS's Macau market share trend, in my opinion. Citi Research even expects Sands China to gain market share in 2026 after previous losses. The Londoner's finished renovations could start to drive better traffic to the key LVS property. A growth performance in line with Wynn's, MGM's, and Galaxy Entertainment's Macau properties would be fairly good for Q4.

LVS Stock Is Fairly Valued

I updated my discounted cash flow (DCF) model for LVS.

I again estimate healthy revenue growth, driven by expansion at Marina Bay Sands and a healthy overall growth outlook in Macau. The 2024-2034 revenue CAGR estimate is set at 5.0% with a growth boost in 2031 from Marina Bay Sands’ expansion. The model assumes 2.5% perpetual growth from 2033 forward.

I assume revenue growth to trickle down well to LVS’s bottom line—I estimate the EBIT margin to increase to 27% in the next few years. LVS’s low tax rate and fairly moderate amount of overall CapEx projects make the cash flow outlook very good for most years. I estimate weaker cash flow for 2028 and 2029 due to LVS’s Marina Bay Sands expansion.

LVS currently owns approximately 74.8% of Sands China. I estimate the remaining 25.2% stake to cause 15% of LVS’s total reported cash flow to minority shareholders of Sands China, accounted for in the model by adding 15% dilution to outstanding shares.

The estimates put LVS’s fair value at $60.9, near the stock price as of writing. I believe that the company’s earnings prospects are fairly priced at the current valuation. The risk-to-reward is not particularly attractive, but not particularly unattractive either.

The fair value estimate is down from $64.4 previously.

CAPM

The DCF model uses a cost of capital [WACC] of 8.37%, derived from a capital asset pricing model:

I estimate a 4.77% interest rate, annualized from Q3 interest expenses and debt. I assume a fairly moderate 30% long-term debt-to-equity ratio, being lower than many other casino operators’ debt leverage.

For the cost of equity, I use the 10-year bond yield of 4.16% as the risk-free rate. The equity risk premium of 4.18% is Professor Aswath Damodaran’s most recent January estimate for the US. I have kept the beta estimate at 0.91. With an ESG addon of 1.5% and a liquidity premium of 0.2%, the cost of equity stands at 9.66% and the WACC at 8.37%.

Takeaway

LVS’s overall outlook is good as Marina Bay Sands continues to grow and as Macau’s gaming industry has a healthy 2026 ahead. The Marina Bay Sands property has shown especially good earnings, meanwhile LVS’s Macau performance isn’t as good as the company has lost some market share. Overall, LVS stock values the company’s earnings prospects fairly. I remain at a Hold rating for now.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: AMD Conviction Report
data.description:
CES keynote

AMD (AMD) just used the CES keynote to make a simple point: it’s no longer a parts vendor, it’s a platform company for AI at rack scale, client, and edge, and it now has credible customer pull to match. The star is Helios, an OCP-wide, double-wide rack reference built with AMD’s next-gen MI455 GPUs (2/3nm, HBM4, 3D chiplets) plus Venice EPYC (Zen 6, up to 256 cores) and Pensando networking. Architecturally, this matters because AMD isn’t just selling accelerators; it’s selling a turnkey, open, Ethernet-first system that can be replicated by OEMs/ODMs, exactly where the industry has been struggling to integrate at scale. If you were waiting for AMD’s “DGX moment,” this is it, only done on open standards (UALink over Ethernet, UEC).

Second, the demand validation didn’t come from slides—it came from customers on stage. OpenAI’s Greg Brockman basically said, “we’re compute-constrained,” endorsed co-design on MI455, and described agentic workloads that balloon inference demand. Luma AI (video/multimodal) said ~60% of their inference already runs on AMD, and they’ll 10x deployments; Fei-Fei Li’s World Labs showed spatial/3D models already optimized on MI325X and pushing to MI450. In healthcare, Illumina/AstraZeneca/Absci laid out production use where memory capacity, bandwidth and inference economics—AMD’s strong suits—are gating factors. This is the right kind of proof: real workloads, not just peak TFLOPS.

Third, AMD extended the roadmap credibility: MI455 this year; MI500 (CDNA 6, 2nm, HBM4E) in 2027, claiming a 1,000× four-year curve. On PCs, Ryzen AI 400 and Ryzen AI Max/Halo target local agents with unified memory and a beefy NPU/GPU; Liquid AI’s lightweight multimodal models give a plausible path to on-device AI that isn’t marketing vapor. That broadens TAM and mix, data center remains the profit engine, but AI PCs/edge add unit volume and stickier ecosystems.

Why it matters competitively: Nvidia (NVDA) still leads in frontier training and end-to-end software, but AMD is closing the system integration gap and leaning into openness. Helios plus ROCm day-0 support and rapid cadence lowers the infrastructure adoption barrier; sovereigns and enterprises that don’t want a closed fabric now have a clear alternative. If AMD can marry HBM4 supply (Samsung/Micron) with rack-level tokens-per-watt and tokens-per-dollar leadership on real models, the share narrative changes from “second source” to “second platform.”

What is the price action telling us?

Source: TradingView

AMD’s still trading a box between 200–205 and 228–230. We’re camped in the upper half (~223–224), which is where breakouts are born or fail. The tell is acceptance above 228–230 on closes, not intraday wicks.

Levels that matter (with Fib confluence):

  • Support ladder: 223/222.9 (61.8%) to 212.8 (78.6%) to 205/200 (range floor).
  • Resistance ladder: 228–230 (box top & 50% retrace) to 237.1 (38.2%) to 245.8 (23.6%) to 260 (prior high).
  • If 260 is reclaimed later, extensions line up at 276/297/320.

TPUs change the tape risk, not the thesis:

So far, I believe AMD’s price has been muted mainly because of Google’s TPUs hype. I believe Google does have a very good strategy for inference, but:

  • Google’s TPUs are a credible in-house alternative that create pricing pressure on merchant GPUs and give hyperscalers bargaining leverage. Near term, that can inject headline volatility right at 228–230 (classic “sell-the-breakout” attempts).
  • But TPUs are capacity, power, and portability-constrained and largely cloud-gated. The broader market still needs flexible, merchant, rack-scale GPUs. That dynamic actually helps AMD as the only open, Ethernet-first #2 platform (Helios/MI455, Venice EPYC, ROCm), while it caps NVDA’s pricing umbrella.

What confirms a real breakout (beyond price):

  • Closes >230 on above-average volume plus improving AMD vs NVDA relative strength on your screen.
  • Headlines that speak to merchant demand, not just TPU rhetoric: Helios/MI455 cluster awards, HBM4 allocation momentum, DOE/Genesis Lux/Discovery progress, and cadence in ROCm day-0 model support. Those turn 230 from a lid into a floor.

Bias & playbook (now incorporating TPU noise):

  • Mildly bullish while above 223/222.9. We’re pressing the top rail; momentum’s trying to turn up.
  • Above 230 (close/hold): bulls in control -> targets 237 → 246 → 260. Expect headline feints around TPUs; treat them as noise unless they come with clear signs of HBM diversion away from MI4xx or AMD guide cuts.
  • Fail at 228–230 and lose 222.9: expect a range rotation to 212.8, possibly the 205/200 reload zone. That doesn’t break the secular story; it just resets the swing while the market digests TPU chatter.

Why I’m leaning long into the trigger:

The fundamental tape, OpenAI multi-GW pact, Helios rack-scale unveiling, ROCm cadence, DOE supercomputers, Sanmina NPI partnership, and HBM4 sourcing progress, argues AMD has graduated from chip vendor to platform supplier. TPUs may pressure pricing, but they also force multi-source strategies, and AMD is the merchant alternative customers need.

Bottom line

Respect the box, but I’m a buyer of confirmation, adding on a daily close >230. If TPU headlines ambush the breakout, your line in the sand is 222.9; below that, let it rotate and reset at 212–205 while the long-term thesis stays intact.

Not Financial Advice

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Delek Logistics Partners: Consistent Distribution Growth Tempered By High Leverage
data.description:
Delek Logistics Partners (DKL) is a midstream master limited partnership that primarily operates in Texas and the surrounding states. The company boasts a long history of growth, which has allowed it to be one of the only midstream partnerships to have increased its distribution every quarter for more than a decade. However, the company’s balance sheet appears to be somewhat weaker than its peers, and its distribution coverage is not as strong. The company is growing rapidly, though, and it boasts a 9.27% yield at the current price, so it might be worth considering for an income portfolio.

About Delek Logistics Partners

Delek Logistics Partners is a midstream master limited partnership that primarily transports crude oil, refined products, natural gas, and water. As is the case with many midstream companies, the infrastructure that is owned by Delek Logistics Partners is primarily located in the states of Texas and Oklahoma, although it also has some operations in Arkansas, Louisiana, and New Mexico:

It is not surprising that the company’s infrastructure is primarily located in these states. After all, both Texas and Oklahoma have been the center of the oil and gas industry in the United States for many years. The Permian Basin, located in Texas, is one of the largest hydrocarbon reservoirs in the world, with the most recent estimates stating that the Permian Basin has technically recoverable resources of more than 70 billion barrels of crude oil. The Permian Basin is the largest known deposit of hydrocarbon resources in the continental United States, and despite being exploited since the 1920s, it still contains substantial quantities of resources. The Permian Basin is also one of the most cost-effective basins in the United States in which to produce crude oil. These factors combined have made this region the center of the country’s oil and gas industry, and given the fact that this single basin accounts for more oil and gas production than any other, it makes a lot of sense for a midstream company to have the bulk of its infrastructure located in the Permian Basin and the surrounding areas.

Another reason why Delek Logistics Partners’ focus on Texas and the surrounding states is that it was originally set up by Delek US Holdings (DK) as a vehicle for transporting crude oil and refined products to and from Delek’s refineries. Delek US Holdings owns four refineries in the United States, which are located in Tyler, Texas; Big Spring, Texas; El Dorado, Arkansas; and Krotz Springs, Louisiana. Delek has no refineries outside of those four, so given DKL’s relationship with Delek, it makes sense that DKL’s operations would mostly be in the areas surrounding those four refineries. This is exactly what we see with the company’s operating footprint.

The fact that Delek Logistics Partners was originally established by Delek US Holdings may give some investors concerns about its risks. After all, back in the COVID-19 pandemic, we saw several midstream partnerships that had a larger corporate parent get merged into the corporate parent and potentially hand investors in the partnership losses. BP Midstream Partners was one example of this, as that partnership conducted its IPO in 2017 at a price of $18 per unit, but it was ultimately acquired by BP (BP) for 0.575 ADR shares of BP, which was $17.077 per unit on the April 5, 2022, closing date of the acquisition. There have also been other midstream companies that got rolled back up into the corporate parent over the years, including Rattler Midstream and Shell Midstream. This is not the only risk that investors need to be concerned about when considering purchasing the partnership units of an MLP with a corporate parent. Many of these companies derive a significant proportion of their cash flow from the corporate parent. This makes some sense because the corporate parent frequently sets up a partnership specifically to meet its own logistics needs. There are some risks of having a single entity be responsible for a sizable proportion of the midstream company’s cash flow, though. In particular, if the corporate parent encounters financial difficulties, it could result in adverse consequences for the midstream partnership as well, since the parent might have to either sell some of its position in the midstream partnership to raise cash or have difficulty honoring the contracts that it has with the midstream partnership. Master limited partnerships that are completely independent of a larger company, such as Enterprise Products Partners (EPD), are sometimes viewed as safer than those that are highly dependent on a single larger company.

Fortunately for investors who are concerned about situations such as the above, Delek Logistics Partners has been working very hard to reduce its dependence on Delek US Holdings for cash flow. Back in 2023, approximately 59% of Delek Logistics Partners’ EBITDA came from Delek US Holdings. That figure declined to 50% for 2024 and is expected to be down to 30% for the full-year 2025 period:

As of the time of writing, we do not know for certain whether or not the company will achieve its goal of deriving 70% of its EBITDA from third-party sources. As of January 15, 2026, Delek Logistics Partners has not announced the date on which it will release its fourth-quarter 2025 and full-year 2025 results, but if history is any guide, it should be sometime around the end of February. For the first nine months of 2025, Delek Logistics Partners had net revenue of $757.557 million. Of this amount, $371.420 million came from Delek US Holdings, and $386.137 million came from outside companies. That is equal to 49.03% from Delek US Holdings. Unless Delek Logistics Partners has substantially higher margins from its third-party contracts than it does from its contracts with Delek US Holdings, it appears to be having problems diversifying its cash flow as much as it expects to. Ultimately, though, money is fungible, and it does not really matter whether the company receives money from Delek US Holdings or an outside company as long as everyone remains financially able to honor the contracts that they have with Delek Logistics Partners. As of the time of writing, there are no fears that customers will be defaulting on their contracts with midstream companies to any significant degree. Many, if not most, upstream and downstream companies are in stronger financial positions than they have been in for many years, and as such, the company’s customers (regardless of whether they are an affiliated party or a third-party company) should be able to continue to pay the money that they have promised to.

In fact, Delek Logistics Partners has been seeing its cash flow increase significantly in recent quarters. For the third quarter of 2025, the company reported an EBITDA of $102.0 million, which represents a massive 47.40% increase over the $69.2 million that the company reported for the third quarter of 2024. One of the reasons for this is that Delek’s crude oil gathering volumes in the Delaware Basin (part of the Permian) set a new record for the third quarter of 2025 with an average of 153,745 barrels of crude oil per day. In the third quarter of 2024, the company’s crude oil gathering pipelines only handled an average of 125,123 barrels of crude oil per day, so the company benefited from a 22.88% increase in gathered crude oil volumes year-over-year.

Delek Logistics’ gathered crude oil volumes are not the only area in which the company has seen improvement compared to the prior-year quarter. We can see that in this chart:

We see that the Midland and the Delaware gathering systems both saw substantial increases year-over-year, with the Delaware gathering system’s volumes setting a new record. The company’s wastewater disposal business is also doing very well, with volumes almost doubling over the past year. This is in line with comments that Delek Logistics Partners’ management made in its third-quarter earnings press release:

During the third quarter Delek Logistics continued its strong execution by making progress on the development of sour gas gathering and acid gas injection capabilities. Delek Logistics also had record crude gathering volumes in its Delaware Business.

The removal of wastewater is a function that many investors do not often associate with midstream companies, even though many of them do it. The oil and gas industry produces a lot of water when engaging in hydraulic fracturing and drilling operations that contain environmental pollutants such as oil, grease, or other chemicals. Environmental regulations require proper disposal of this water, and Delek Logistics Partners provides this service to its customers. This business has been a source of growth for Delek Logistics Partners over the past few years, with its wastewater gathering operations growing from an average of 72,000 barrels per day in 2022 to 616,000 barrels per day in 2025 (a figure that the company expects to increase to 657,000 barrels per day by the end of 2025):

We should note that this has not been entirely organic growth, as some of it was driven by an acquisition that Delek Logistics Partners made last year. On January 2, 2025, Delek Logistics Partners announced that it acquired Gravity Water Intermediate Holdings for $209.3 million in cash, along with 2.175 million common partnership units (valued at about $91 million at the time that the deal was consummated). The partnership units that the company paid for this acquisition are actually worth a bit more now, as the current unit price of $48.14 puts the value of 2.175 million units at $104.7045 million.

The acquisition of Gravity Water Intermediate Holdings had an immediate positive impact on the cash flow and profitability of Delek Logistics Partners. The obvious reason for that is that Gravity Water was cash flow positive at the time of the acquisition, but the benefits extend beyond that. Delek Logistics Partners actually managed to get a pretty good price for the acquisition. The purchase price that Delek Logistics Partners paid was 5.5 times Gravity Water’s EBITDA, which, as regular readers likely know, is in line with the payback period that midstream companies usually attempt to get on their capital investments. For example, this is in line with the 4x to 6x EBITDA multiple that midstream companies typically try to get whenever they are planning to build a new pipeline.

Financial Considerations

Delek Logistics Partners has an incredibly high debt-to-equity ratio, which could pose risks for investors. As of September 30, 2025 (the most recent date for which data is currently available), the company had $2.2890 billion in net debt against $17.5 million in partners’ equity. That is a whopping debt-to-equity ratio of 130.8. As we can see here, this is substantially higher than the figures possessed by some of the other midstream partnerships and corporations in the market:

Company Name

Net Debt-to-Equity Ratio

Delek Logistics Partners

130.8

Enterprise Products Partners

1.12

Energy Transfer (ET)

1.28

MPLX (MPLX)

1.68

Kinder Morgan (KMI)

1.02

The Williams Companies (WMB)

1.88

Targa Resources (TRGP)

6.11

As shown, Delek Logistics Partners has a substantially higher net debt-to-equity ratio than do any of its peer companies. Indeed, even the heavily leveraged Targa Resources is much more conservatively financed than Delek Logistics Partners. This could be a sign that the company is too reliant on debt to finance itself, as the lower ratios of the peer companies suggest that a greater balance between debt and equity financing is generally advisable for risk management purposes.

However, we should note that Delek Logistics Partners has had even weaker balance sheets in the past. As shown here, up until 2024, the company actually had a negative shareholders’ equity:

(all figures in millions of U.S. dollars)

A negative partner’ equity symbolizes an undesirable situation in which a company’s liabilities exceed the value of its assets. This is typically the result of losses accumulated over time, which is not necessarily surprising for midstream companies. After all, over the past decade, there were a few instances in which pipeline and energy infrastructure owners wrote down the value of some of their assets. This was an especially common occurrence during the COVID-19 pandemic, for example. With that said, Delek Logistics Partners reported positive net income during all four quarters of 2020, which was much better than some of its peers achieved.

There are other things that can cause negative equity, including distribution payments that exceed the company’s profits. This appears to be the reason for the negative equity in the case of Delek Logistics Partners. This chart shows the company’s annual net income for every year since 2016:

(all figures in millions of U.S. dollars)

This chart shows the distributions that Delek Logistics Partners paid out over the same period:

(all figures in millions of U.S. dollars)

As we can see, Delek Logistics Partners’ distributions pretty consistently exceed its net income in most years. This does not necessarily mean that the company cannot afford its distributions, however. As midstream companies operate in a capital-intensive and asset-intensive industry, they tend to have high depreciation and amortization expenses. These expenses reduce their reported net income, but they do not actually represent cash leaving the business. A distribution is paid using cash flow, not accounting net income, and looking solely at net income provides a misleading picture of how well a company can afford its distribution.

By the same token, Delek Logistics Partners repays its debt using cash flow, not net income. For the third quarter of 2025, the partnership reported an EBITDA (a proxy for pre-tax cash flow) of $101.978 million. If we annualize that figure, we get $407.912 million. If the company were to use all of its EBITDA to pay down its debt, it would take about 5.61 years. That is still a pretty high figure for a midstream partnership, as companies like Enterprise Products Partners and MPLX have net debt-to-adjusted EBITDA ratios of less than 4.0x. However, it is not as bad of a situation as we might think from a simple look at the company’s net debt to equity ratio.

Distribution Analysis

Delek Logistics Partners currently pays a distribution of $1.120 per unit quarterly, which gives the company a massive 9.27% yield at the current share price. The most recent distribution is an increase over the $1.115 per share distribution that it paid for the second quarter of 2025, and it represents the company’s 51st consecutive quarterly distribution increase:

This is something of a rarity among midstream partnerships. Unlike some of the other companies in the sector, Delek Logistics Partners did not cut its distribution following the industry crises that occurred following the crude oil price collapses in 2014 and 2020. In fact, it has one of the best track records of any midstream partnership as far as its distribution is concerned. However, the fact that the company has raised its distribution every quarter for more than ten straight years does not necessarily mean that it can actually afford the distribution that it pays out. Let us take a closer look at its finances to see how well it is covering the payout.

For the third quarter of 2025, Delek Logistics Partners reported that it had a distributable cash flow of $73.584 million. That was sufficient to cover the distribution that the company declared 1.23 times over, which is, admittedly, tighter than we would really like to see. Wall Street analysts usually consider anything higher than 1.20x to be sustainable over the long term, and Delek Logistics Partners does meet that criterion. However, I am somewhat more conservative than Wall Street analysts and typically like to see this ratio be above 1.30x in order to add a margin of safety to the position. Many of the partnerships that are recommended here at Energy Profits in Dividends have distribution coverage ratios of 1.30x or higher. Delek Logistics Partners does not satisfy this requirement, and as such, there may be higher risks to the distribution here than there would be with some other midstream companies. Potential investors may want to keep this in mind before purchasing the partnership units of this company.

Valuation

Delek Logistics Partners does not appear to be particularly expensive right now. Seeking Alpha’s Quant rating system assigns the company a valuation grade of B:

However, we do notice that some of the company’s valuation ratios are significantly above the energy sector median. In particular, the enterprise value-to-EBITDA is rather high. Here is how it compares to some of the peer companies:

In short, Delek Logistics Partners does not appear to be overpriced at the current level.

Conclusion

In conclusion, Delek Logistics Partners is a high-yielding midstream partnership that has a long history of increasing its distribution on a quarterly basis. These quarterly increases may be particularly attractive to those income-seeking investors who wish to ensure that their incomes keep up with inflation. However, the company is quite highly leveraged, and its distribution coverage is not as strong as some of its peers, so there could be some risks here. The company has historically been fairly good at delivering growth, however, and its valuation is reasonable, so it might be worth considering for an income portfolio.
